sections.heading: Considerations
sections.body:
Before you enable Encryption at Rest using AKV (Azure Key Vault) over private endpoints,
review the following use cases, benefits, limitations, and prerequisites.

sections.heading: Use Case
sections.body:
Suppose your Atlas deployment is on a single cloud service provider.
You now have a requirement that all access to your AKV (Azure Key Vault) occur over your
cloud provider's private networking infrastructure. This page walks you
through the steps to enable private endpoint connections for your
Atlas project.

sections.heading: Benefits
sections.body:
You can use the Atlas Administration API to allow Atlas to configure
Encryption At Rest with AKV (Azure Key Vault) using private endpoints. This allows all
traffic to AKV (Azure Key Vault) to pass through a set of private endpoints and avoid
exposing AKV (Azure Key Vault) to the public internet or public IP addresses. It
eliminates the need to maintain allowed IP addresses while enhancing
data security by keeping all AKV (Azure Key Vault) traffic within Azure (Microsoft Azure)'s private
network.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.body:
- Atlas doesn't support Encryption at Rest using CMK (customer-managed key) over
  private endpoints for multi-cloud deployments. If you enable encryption
  at rest using CMK (customer-managed key) over Azure Private Link in an existing project with
  multi-cloud clusters, Atlas disables the multi-cloud
  clusters in your project.

- Atlas doesn't support Encryption at Rest using CMK (customer-managed key) over
  private endpoints for projects in the `INACTIVE` state.

- Once you've [Enable and Set up Private Endpoint Connections for a Project](azure-kms-enable-pvt-endpoint) to your AKV (Azure Key Vault), you can't modify
  the **Key Vault Name**, **Subscription ID**, or **Resource Group Name** of your AKV (Azure Key Vault), but you can still [rotate the Microsoft Azure Key Identifier](rotate-azure-key-identifier).
  To modify these values, you must first [remove](azure-remove-reject-pvt-endpoint).

sections.heading: Multi-Region Clusters
sections.body:
If you are using a multi-region cluster, you must create a private endpoint for
your AKV (Azure Key Vault) in **each region** where your cluster is deployed.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
To enable customer-managed keys with AKV (Azure Key Vault) for a MongoDB project, you
must:

- Use an M10 or larger cluster.

* Have the Azure (Microsoft Azure) account and Key Vault credentials, and the key
  identifier for the encryption key in your AKV (Azure Key Vault).

  - For the account, you must have the client ID, tenant ID, and
    secret. To learn about a secretless option, see [Manage Customer Keys with Azure (Microsoft Azure) Key Vault (Secretless Authentication)](security-azure-kms-secretless).

  - For the Key Vault, you must have the subscription ID, Resource Group
    Name, and Key Vault name.

  To learn how to configure these Azure components, see the
  [Azure Documentation](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/index).

  Atlas uses these resources when enabling encryption at rest
  for a cluster in the Atlas project.

- If your **App registrations** use [conditional access
  policies](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/conditional-access/workload-identity),
  which is uncommon, you must [Atlas IP addresses](atlas-add-inbound-ips) and [the public
  IP addresses of your cluster nodes](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-getgroupipaddresses) for app registration. This is used
  only for app registration and not used for key vault access.

> **Note:** You must register **Microsoft.Network** under your Azure (Microsoft Azure)
> subscription resource providers. To learn more, see [Azure
> documentation](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-resource-manager/management/resource-providers-and-types).

sections.heading: Enable Customer-Managed Keys for a Project
sections.body:
You must enable CMK (customer-managed key) for a project before you can enable it on a
cluster in that project. You can enable CMK (customer-managed key) for a project from the
Atlas UI and Atlas Administration API.**Ui:**Atlas UIAtlas supports two authentication methods for AKV (Azure Key Vault):* **Service Principal (Recommended)**: Use an Atlas-managed
  service principal specific to your project to authenticate to
  AKV (Azure Key Vault) (secretless authentication). To learn more, see
  [Manage Customer Keys with Azure (Microsoft Azure) Key Vault (Secretless Authentication)](security-azure-kms-secretless).

* **Static Credentials**: Provide customer-managed credentials
  (covered here).| Client ID         | Enter the Client ID (or Application ID) of the Azure application. Ensure that you have assigned the Active Directory Application the necessary access. To learn more, see Required Access. |
| ----------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Tenant ID         | Enter the Tenant ID (or Directory ID) of the Active Directory tenant.                                                                                                                      |
| Secret            | Enter one of the application's non-expired Client Secrets associated with the Active Directory tenant.                                                                                     |
| Azure Environment | Select the Azure cloud your Active Directory tenant lives in.                                                                                                                              || Subscription ID     | Enter the Subscription ID of the Key Vault.                                                                                       |
| ------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Resource Group Name | Enter the Resource Group name of an Azure Resource Group containing the Key Vault.                                                |
| Key Vault Name      | Enter the name of the Key Vault. Ensure that the Key Vault has the necessary Access Policies. To learn more, see Required Access. |> **Note:** You can't modify the AKV (Azure Key Vault) credentials here after you
> [Enable and Set up Private Endpoint Connections for a Project](azure-kms-enable-pvt-endpoint) to your AKV (Azure Key Vault).| Key Identifier | Enter the full URL (Uniform Resource Locator) for the key created in the Key Vault.IMPORTANT: The key identifier must be provided in the full Azure general format:https\://\{keyvault-name}.vault.azure.net/\{object-type}/\{object-name}You may append /\{object-version}. However, we recommend you omit the version to enable automatic resolution to the latest version. |
| -------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |To learn more, see [Enable and Set up Private Endpoint Connections for a Project](azure-kms-enable-pvt-endpoint)If you configured Atlas using the Atlas Administration API to
communicate with AKV (Azure Key Vault) using Azure Private Link to ensure that all traffic
between Atlas and Key Vault takes place over Azure (Microsoft Azure)'s
private network interfaces, Atlas sets the Require
Private Networking status to Active. If the status is
Inactive, you can, optionally, complete the steps to
[Enable and Set up Private Endpoint Connections for a Project](azure-kms-enable-pvt-endpoint) if you want Atlas to use
private endpoint connections for your AKV (Azure Key Vault).Atlas displays a banner in the Atlas console during the
encryption process.**Api:**Atlas Admin API> **Note:** This `curl` command uses a service account access token ([OAuth 2.0](https://oauth.net/2/)) to authenticate
> instead of API keys. To learn more, see [API Access Lists endpoint](atlas-admin-api-access).> **Example:** 
>
> ```json
> curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
> --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-05-30+json" \
> --header "Content-Type: application/vnd.atlas.2024-05-30+json" \
> --request PATCH "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/66c9e8f1dd6c9960802420e9/encryptionAtRest" \
> --data '
> {
>    "azureKeyVault": {
>       "azureEnvironment": "AZURE",
>       "clientID": "b054a9ff-b60a-4cb6-8df6-20726eaefce6",
>       "enabled": true,
>       "keyIdentifier": "https://test-tf-export.vault.azure.net/keys/test/78b9134f9bc94fda8027a32b4715bf3f",
>       "keyVaultName": "test-tf-export",
>       "resourceGroupName": "test-tf-export",
>       "secret": "",
>       "subscriptionID": "009774e0-124f-4a69-83e0-ca8cd8acb4e2",
>       "tenantID": "1f6ef922-9303-402a-bae2-cc68810b023c"
>    }
> }'
> ```> **Note:** You can't modify the following settings after you [Enable and Set up Private Endpoint Connections for a Project](azure-kms-enable-pvt-endpoint) to your AKV (Azure Key Vault):
>
> - `keyVaultName`
>
> - `resourceGroupName`
>
> - `subscriptionID`To verify your request to enable and configure encryption at rest
using the keys you manage using AKV (Azure Key Vault), send a `GET` request to
the `encryptionAtRest` [endpoint](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-getgroupencryptionatrest).> **Example:** **Input:**
>
> ```json
> curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
> --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-05-30+json" \
> --header "Content-Type: application/vnd.atlas.2024-05-30+json" \
> --include \
> --request GET "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest"
> ```
>
> **Output:**
>
> ```shell
> {
>   "azureKeyVault": {
>     "azureEnvironment": "AZURE",
>     "clientID": "632ff709-32a8-48a3-8224-30d2386fadaf",
>     "enabled": true,
>     "keyIdentifier": "https://EXAMPLEKeyVault.vault.azure.net/keys/EXAMPLEKey/d891821e3d364e9eb88fbd3d11807b86",
>     "keyVaultName": "string",
>     "requirePrivateNetworking": false,
>     "resourceGroupName": "string",
>     "subscriptionID": "a39012fb-d604-4cd1-8841-77f705f3e6d5",
>     "tenantID": "ee46317d-36a3-4472-a3dd-6549e901da0b",
>     "valid": true
>   }
> }
> ```In the response, `enabled` is `true` if your project is
successfully enabled for Encryption at Rest using CMK (customer-managed key). You can
set up private networking to ensure that all traffic between
Atlas and Key Vault takes place over Azure (Microsoft Azure)'s private network
interfaces. To learn more, see [Enable and Set up Private Endpoint Connections for a Project](azure-kms-enable-pvt-endpoint).

codeBlocks.content:
curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
--header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-05-30+json" \
--header "Content-Type: application/vnd.atlas.2024-05-30+json" \
--request PATCH "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/66c9e8f1dd6c9960802420e9/encryptionAtRest" \
--data '
{
   "azureKeyVault": {
      "azureEnvironment": "AZURE",
      "clientID": "b054a9ff-b60a-4cb6-8df6-20726eaefce6",
      "enabled": true,
      "keyIdentifier": "https://test-tf-export.vault.azure.net/keys/test/78b9134f9bc94fda8027a32b4715bf3f",
      "keyVaultName": "test-tf-export",
      "resourceGroupName": "test-tf-export",
      "secret": "",
      "subscriptionID": "009774e0-124f-4a69-83e0-ca8cd8acb4e2",
      "tenantID": "1f6ef922-9303-402a-bae2-cc68810b023c"
   }
}'

sections.heading: Enable and Set up Private Endpoint Connections for a Project
sections.body:
You can enable and set up private endpoints by using the Atlas UI and
Atlas Administration API. To enable private networking and set up a private
endpoint in your AKV (Azure Key Vault), you must do the following:**Ui:**Atlas UITo learn more, see
[Enable Customer-Managed Keys for a Project](azure-kms-configure-project-pvt-endpoint).* Toggle Require Private Networking to enable private
  endpoint connections.

* Click Save.The Set Up Private Networking for Azure page displays.If your cluster is already enabled for Encryption at Rest with
AKV (Azure Key Vault), Atlas automatically populates the regions with your
cluster regions. Otherwise, to add regions, do the following:* Select the Azure (Microsoft Azure) regions from the dropdown.

  > **Important:** For multi-region clusters, you must select **each region** where the
  > cluster is deployed.

* Click Continue.

  Atlas automatically creates private endpoints in these
  regions to allow you to connect by using private networking.You can use the Azure (Microsoft Azure) [UI](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/manage-private-endpoint),
[CLI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/network/private-endpoint-connection),
or Terraform to approve the private endpoint connections.After you approve, Atlas automatically migrates all
clusters for which you enabled [customer managed keys](azure-enable-cluster-encryption-at-rest-pvt-endpoint), including
existing clusters that allow connections using public
internet, to use only the private endpoint connection. You can
optionally disable public internet access to your AKV (Azure Key Vault) after
migrating your clusters to use the private endpoint
connection. All new Atlas clusters on Azure (Microsoft Azure) will by
default use only the active private endpoint connection. Atlas
deploys additional nodes for existing clusters only in the
regions with approved private endpoints.After you approve the private endpoint, it can take Atlas up
to three minutes to reflect the current status of your private
endpoint. For private endpoints in each region, the
Approve Endpoints page displays the status of each
private endpoint. To learn more, see
[View Private Endpoints and their Statuses](azure-view-pvt-endpoints).**Apizure-view-pvt-endpoints:**Atlas Admin APISend a `PATCH` request to the [endpoint](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-updateencryptionatrest) and set the `requirePrivateNetworking`
flag value to `true`.> **Note:** This `curl` command uses a service account access token ([OAuth 2.0](https://oauth.net/2/)) to authenticate
> instead of API keys. To learn more, see [API Access Lists endpoint](atlas-admin-api-access).> **Example:** 
>
> ```json
> curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
> --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2023-01-01+json" \
> --header "Content-Type: application/vnd.atlas.2023-01-01+json" \
> --include \
> --request PATCH "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/" \
> --data '
>   {
>     "azureKeyVault": {
>       "azureEnvironment": "AZURE",
>       "clientID": "632ff709-32a8-48a3-8224-30d2386fadaf",
>       "enabled": true,
>       "keyIdentifier": "https://EXAMPLEKeyVault.vault.azure.net/keys/EXAMPLEKey/d891821e3d364e9eb88fbd3d11807b86",
>       "keyVaultName": "string",
>       "requirePrivateNetworking": true,
>       "resourceGroupName": "string",
>       "secret": "string",
>       "subscriptionID": "a39012fb-d604-4cd1-8841-77f705f3e6d5",
>       "tenantID": "ee46317d-36a3-4472-a3dd-6549e901da0b"
>     }
>   }'
> ```Send a `POST` request to the [endpoint](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-createencryptionatrestprivateendpoint) with the Azure (Microsoft Azure)
region in which you want Atlas to create the private
endpoint. You must send a separate request for each region
in which you want Atlas to create a private endpoint.> **Important:** If you are using a multi-region cluster, you must create a private endpoint for
> your AKV (Azure Key Vault) in **each region** where your cluster is deployed.> **Example:** 
>
> ```json
> curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
> --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2023-01-01+json" \
> --header "Content-Type: application/vnd.atlas.2023-01-01+json" \
> --include \
> --request POST "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/AZURE/privateEndpoints" \
> --data '
>   {
>     "regionName": "US_CENTRAL"
>   }'
> ```After you approve the private endpoint, the following restrictions
apply:* Atlas creates all new clusters only in the regions with
  approved private endpoints.

* Atlas deploys additional nodes for existing clusters only
  in the regions with approved private endpoints.You can use the Azure (Microsoft Azure) [UI](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/manage-private-endpoint),
[CLI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/network/private-endpoint-connection), or
Terraform to approve the private endpoint connections.After you approve, Atlas automatically migrates all
clusters for which you enabled [customer managed keys](azure-enable-cluster-encryption-at-rest-pvt-endpoint), including
existing clusters that allow connections using public
internet, to use only the private endpoint connection. You can
optionally disable public internet access to your AKV (Azure Key Vault) after
migrating your clusters to use the private endpoint
connection. All new Atlas clusters on Azure (Microsoft Azure) will by
default use only the active private endpoint connection.To check the status of the private endpoint, send a `GET`
request to the `encryptionAtRest` [endpoint](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-listgroupencryptionatrestprivateendpoints).> **Example:** **Input:**
>
> ```json
> curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
> --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2023-01-01+json" \
> --header "Content-Type: application/vnd.atlas.2023-01-01+json" \
> --include \
> --request GET "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/AZURE/privateEndpoints"
> ```
>
> **Output:**
>
> ```shell
> {
>   "links": [
>     {
>       "href": "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas",
>       "rel": "self"
>     }
>   ],
>   "results": [
>     {
>       "cloudProvider": "AZURE",
>       "errorMessage": "string",
>       "id": "24-hexadecimal-digit-string",
>       "regionName": "string",
>       "status": "INITIATING",
>       "privateEndpointConnectionName": "string"
>     }
>   ],
>   "totalCount": 0
> }
> ```After you approve the private endpoint, it can take Atlas up
to three minutes to reflect the current status of your private
endpoint. The private endpoint can have one of the following
statuses:| INITATING           | Indicates that Atlas is in the process of creating the private endpoint.                                                                              |
| ------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| PENDING\_ACCEPTANCE | Indicates that the private endpoint hasn't yet been approved. You must accept the private endpoint to allow Atlas to use it.                          |
| ACTIVE              | Indicates that the private endpoint is approved and Atlas can or is using it.                                                                         |
| PENDING\_RECREATION | Indicates that the private endpoint was either rejected or removed and Atlas is in the process of creating a new private endpoint in the same region. |
| FAILED              | Indicates that the private endpoint creation failed.                                                                                                  |
| DELETING            | Indicates that Atlas is in the process of deleting the private endpoint.                                                                              |After you enable Encryption at Rest Using CMK (customer-managed key) (Over Private
Networking) for your project, you can enable [customer managed keys](azure-enable-cluster-encryption-at-rest-pvt-endpoint) using CMK (customer-managed key) for
each Atlas cluster in your project.

codeBlocks.content:
curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
--header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2023-01-01+json" \
--header "Content-Type: application/vnd.atlas.2023-01-01+json" \
--include \
--request PATCH "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/" \
--data '
  {
    "azureKeyVault": {
      "azureEnvironment": "AZURE",
      "clientID": "632ff709-32a8-48a3-8224-30d2386fadaf",
      "enabled": true,
      "keyIdentifier": "https://EXAMPLEKeyVault.vault.azure.net/keys/EXAMPLEKey/d891821e3d364e9eb88fbd3d11807b86",
      "keyVaultName": "string",
      "requirePrivateNetworking": true,
      "resourceGroupName": "string",
      "secret": "string",
      "subscriptionID": "a39012fb-d604-4cd1-8841-77f705f3e6d5",
      "tenantID": "ee46317d-36a3-4472-a3dd-6549e901da0b"
    }
  }'

sections.heading: Enable Customer Key Management for an Atlas Cluster
sections.body:
After you [Enable Customer-Managed Keys for a Project](azure-kms-configure-project), you must enable customer key
management for each Atlas cluster that contains data that you want
to encrypt. If you configured private endpoint connections for your
Atlas project, Atlas automatically migrates all clusters for
which you already enabled [customer managed keys](azure-enable-cluster-encryption-at-rest-pvt-endpoint), including
existing clusters that allow connections using public internet, to
use only the private endpoint connection.

> **Note:** You must have the [Project Owner](<Project Owner>) role to
> enable customer key management for clusters in that project.

For new clusters, toggle the [Manage Your Own Encryption Keys](create-cluster-enable-encryption) setting to **Yes**, if it
isn't already enabled, when you create the cluster.

For existing clusters:

For the cluster that contains data that you want to encrypt, click the
, then select Edit Configuration.

- Expand the Additional Settings panel.

- Toggle the Manage your own encryption keys setting to
  Yes.

- Verify the status of the Require Private Networking
  setting for your cluster.

  If you configured Encryption at Rest Using CMK (customer-managed key) (Over Private
  Networking) for Atlas at the project level, the status is
  Active. If you haven't configured any private
  endpoint connection for your project, the status is
  Inactive.

* Click Review Changes.

* Review your changes, then click Apply Changes to update
  your cluster.

sections.heading: Disable Customer-Managed Keys for a Project
sections.body:
To disable CMK (customer-managed key) for a project, you must first [remove](azure-remove-reject-pvt-endpoint) all private endpoints associated
with the project, regardless of their state. Atlas displays an error if
you attempt to disable CMK (customer-managed key) for a project that is associated with
active private endpoints.

After removing all private endpoints for a project, you must disable
customer key management on each cluster in the project before you
disable the feature for the project.

> **Warning:** Do *not* disable or delete any AKV (Azure Key Vault) keys that any cluster in your
> Atlas project uses before you have disabled customer key
> management within the Atlas project. If Atlas can't access
> an AKV (Azure Key Vault) key, any data that the key encrypted becomes inaccessible.

sections.heading: Create New Endpoints for a Project
sections.body:
After enabling and setting up private endpoint connections for your
project, you can add additional endpoints at anytime from the
Atlas UI and the Atlas Administration API.**Ui:**Atlas UI* Select the regions from the dropdown.

* Click Continue.

  Atlas automatically creates private endpoints in these
  regions to allow you to connect by using private networking.You must approve each endpoint in your AKV (Azure Key Vault) to use the private
endpoint. You can use the Azure (Microsoft Azure) [UI](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/manage-private-endpoint),
[CLI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/network/private-endpoint-connection),
or Terraform to approve the private endpoint connections.For private endpoints in each region, the Approve
Endpoints page displays the status of each private endpoint.
After you approve the private endpoint, it can take Atlas up
to three minutes to reflect the current status of your private
endpoint. To learn more about private endpoint statuses, see
[View Private Endpoints and their Statuses](azure-view-pvt-endpoints).**Api:**Atlas Admin APISend a `POST` request to the [endpoint](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-createencryptionatrestprivateendpoint) with the Azure (Microsoft Azure)
region in which you want Atlas to create the private
endpoint. You must send a separate request for each region
in which you want Atlas to create a private endpoint.> **Note:** This `curl` command uses a service account access token ([OAuth 2.0](https://oauth.net/2/)) to authenticate
> instead of API keys. To learn more, see [API Access Lists endpoint](atlas-admin-api-access).> **Example:** 
>
> ```json
> curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
> --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2023-01-01+json" \
> --header "Content-Type: application/vnd.atlas.2023-01-01+json" \
> --include \
> --request POST "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/AZURE/privateEndpoints" \
> --data '
>   {
>     "regionName": "US_CENTRAL"
>   }'
> ```You can use the Azure (Microsoft Azure) [UI](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/manage-private-endpoint),
[CLI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/network/private-endpoint-connection), or
Terraform to approve the private endpoint connections.To check the status of the private endpoint, send a `GET`
request to the `encryptionAtRest` [endpoint](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-listgroupencryptionatrestprivateendpoints).> **Example:** **Input:**
>
> ```json
> curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
> --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2023-01-01+json" \
> --header "Content-Type: application/vnd.atlas.2023-01-01+json" \
> --include \
> --request GET "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/AZURE/privateEndpoints"
> ```
>
> **Output:**
>
> ```shell
> {
>   "links": [
>     {
>       "href": "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas",
>       "rel": "self"
>     }
>   ],
>   "results": [
>     {
>       "cloudProvider": "AZURE",
>       "errorMessage": "string",
>       "id": "24-hexadecimal-digit-string",
>       "regionName": "string",
>       "status": "INITIATING",
>       "privateEndpointConnectionName": "string"
>     }
>   ],
>   "totalCount": 0
> }
> ```After you approve the private endpoint, it can take Atlas up
to three minutes to reflect the current status of your private
endpoint. The private endpoint can have one of the following
statuses:| INITATING           | Indicates that Atlas is in the process of creating the private endpoint.                                                                              |
| ------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| PENDING\_ACCEPTANCE | Indicates that the private endpoint hasn't yet been approved. You must accept the private endpoint to allow Atlas to use it.                          |
| ACTIVE              | Indicates that the private endpoint is approved and Atlas can or is using it.                                                                         |
| PENDING\_RECREATION | Indicates that the private endpoint was either rejected or removed and Atlas is in the process of creating a new private endpoint in the same region. |
| FAILED              | Indicates that the private endpoint creation failed.                                                                                                  |
| DELETING            | Indicates that Atlas is in the process of deleting the private endpoint.                                                                              |

sections.heading: Reject or Remove Private Endpoint Connection
sections.body:
You can remove private endpoint connections from the Atlas UI and
the Atlas Administration API.**Ui:**Atlas UI* Click the  icon in the Actions
  column for the private endpoint that you want to remove.

* Click Delete to confirm deletion of the private
  endpoint.**Api:**Atlas Admin APITo remove a private endpoint, send a `DELETE` request to
the Atlas Administration API [endpoint](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-requestencryptionatrestprivateendpointdeletion) and specify the ID of
the project and of the private endpoint that you want to delete. You can
retrieve the ID of the private endpoint that you want to delete by sending
a `GET` request to the Atlas Administration API [Return One Private
Endpoint Service for One Provider](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-getgroupprivateendpointservice) endpoint.> **Note:** This `curl` command uses a service account access token ([OAuth 2.0](https://oauth.net/2/)) to authenticate
> instead of API keys. To learn more, see [API Access Lists endpoint](atlas-admin-api-access).> **Example:** 
>
> ```json
> curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
> --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-10-23+json" \\ or a different version of the Atlas Admin API
> --header "Content-Type: application/json" \
> --include \
> --request DELETE "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/AZURE/privateEndpoints/{endpointId}" \
> --data '
> {
>    "cloudProvider": "AZURE",
>    "regions": [
>       "string"
>    ]
> }'
> ```When you delete a private endpoint, the private endpoint transitions to
the `DELETING` status while Atlas deletes the private endpoint.If you remove or reject an active private endpoint from the Azure (Microsoft Azure) UI,
Atlas automatically attempts to recreate a new private endpoint in
the same region. You can check the status of the private endpoint from
the Atlas UI and the Atlas Administration API. To learn more, see
[View Private Endpoints and their Statuses](azure-view-pvt-endpoints).While Atlas attempts to create a new private endpoint, the status of
the private endpoint that you rejected or removed transitions to
`PENDING_RECREATION` and the new endpoint that Atlas attempts to
create is in `INITIATING` state. You must approve the new private
endpoint after it is created.

codeBlocks.content:
curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
--header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-10-23+json" \\ or a different version of the Atlas Admin API
--header "Content-Type: application/json" \
--include \
--request DELETE "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/AZURE/privateEndpoints/{endpointId}" \
--data '
{
   "cloudProvider": "AZURE",
   "regions": [
      "string"
   ]
}'

sections.heading: View Private Endpoints and their Statuses
sections.body:
You can view the private endpoints in the various regions and their
statuses from the Atlas UI and the Atlas Administration API.**Ui:**Atlas UIEach private endpoint can be in one of the following statuses:| PENDING APPROVAL | Indicates that the private endpoint hasn't yet been approved. You must accept the private endpoint to allow Atlas to use it. |
| ---------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ACTIVE           | Indicates that the private endpoint is approved and Atlas can or is using it.                                                |
| FAILED           | Indicates that the private endpoint creation failed.                                                                         |**Api:**Atlas Admin APIYou can view the private endpoint and their statuses from the
Atlas Administration API by sending a `GET` request to the
Atlas Administration API `encryptionAtRest`
[get all](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-listgroupencryptionatrestprivateendpoints)
endpoint or [get one](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-getgroupencryptionatrestprivateendpoint)
endpoint, for which you must specify the ID of the private endpoint
in the path.> **Note:** This `curl` command uses a service account access token ([OAuth 2.0](https://oauth.net/2/)) to authenticate
> instead of API keys. To learn more, see [API Access Lists endpoint](atlas-admin-api-access).> **Example:** 
>
> Return All Private Endpoints for One Project
>
> ```
> curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
> --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-10-23+json" \\ or a different version of the Atlas Admin API
> --header "Content-Type: application/json" \
> --include \
> --request GET "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/AZURE/privateEndpoints/"
> ```Each private endpoint can be in one of the following statuses:| INITATING           | Indicates that Atlas is in the process of creating the private endpoint.                                                                              |
| ------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| PENDING\_ACCEPTANCE | Indicates that the private endpoint hasn't yet been approved. You must accept the private endpoint to allow Atlas to use it.                          |
| ACTIVE              | Indicates that the private endpoint is approved and Atlas can or is using it.                                                                         |
| PENDING\_RECREATION | Indicates that the private endpoint was either rejected or removed and Atlas is in the process of creating a new private endpoint in the same region. |
| FAILED              | Indicates that the private endpoint creation failed.                                                                                                  |
| DELETING            | Indicates that Atlas is in the process of deleting the private endpoint.                                                                              |

codeBlocks.content:
curl --header "Authorization: Bearer {ACCESS-TOKEN}" \
--header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-10-23+json" \\ or a different version of the Atlas Admin API
--header "Content-Type: application/json" \
--include \
--request GET "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/encryptionAtRest/AZURE/privateEndpoints/"

sections.heading: Disable Private Endpoint Connections for a Project
sections.body:
To disable private endpoint connections for a project, you must first
[remove](azure-remove-reject-pvt-endpoint) all private endpoints,
associated with the project, regardless of their state. Atlas
doesn't disable private endpoint connections for a project if the
project is associated with active private endpoints.After removing all private endpoints for a project, you can disable
private endpoint connections for the project by using the Atlas UI
and the Atlas Administration API.**Ui:**Atlas UI* Under Encryption at Rest using your Key Management, select Azure Key Vault.

* Under Network Settings, toggle the button next to Require Private
  Networking to  Off.

* Click Save.**Api:**Atlas Admin APITo disable a private endpoint connection, send a
`PATCH` request to the [endpoint](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-updateencryptionatrest) with the `requirePrivateNetworking` boolean
flag value set to `false`.> **Example:** 
>
> ```shell
> {
>   "azureKeyVault": {
>     "azureEnvironment": "AZURE",
>     "clientID": "632ff709-32a8-48a3-8224-30d2386fadaf",
>     "enabled": true,
>     "keyIdentifier": "https://EXAMPLEKeyVault.vault.azure.net/keys/EXAMPLEKey/d891821e3d364e9eb88fbd3d11807b86",
>     "keyVaultName": "string",
>     "requirePrivateNetworking": false,
>     "resourceGroupName": "string",
>     "secret": "string",
>     "subscriptionID": "a39012fb-d604-4cd1-8841-77f705f3e6d5",
>     "tenantID": "ee46317d-36a3-4472-a3dd-6549e901da0b"
>   }
> }
> ```

sections.heading: Revoke Access to an Encryption Key
sections.body:
You can revoke Atlas's access to an encryption key from within
AKV (Azure Key Vault) to freeze your data. Atlas automatically pauses your
clusters when you revoke access to the encryption key.

sections.heading: Rotate your Azure Key Identifier
sections.body:
> **Note:** This feature is not available for any of the following deployments:
>
> - Free clusters
>
> - Flex clusters
>
> To learn more, see [Limits](limits).

Before you begin, learn [About Rotating Your Azure Key Identifier](azure-kms-rotate-key).

You must create a new key in the AKV (Azure Key Vault) associated with your
Atlas project. The following procedure documents how to rotate your
Atlas project Key Identifier by specifying a new key identifier in
Atlas.

- Click Azure Key Vault if the Azure Key Vault
  selector is not already active.

- Click Encryption Key if the Encryption Key
  selector is not already active.

- Enter the Azure Key Identifier in the Key Identifier
  field.

  Include the full URL (Uniform Resource Locator) to the new encryption key identifier. For
  example:

  ```http
  https://mykeyvault.vault.azure.net/keys/AtlasKMSKey/a241124e3d364e9eb99fbd3e11124b23
  ```

  > **Important:** The encryption key **must** belong to the Key Vault configured
  > for the project. Click the Key Vault section to view
  > the currently configured Key Vault for the project.

- Click Update Credentials.

Atlas displays a banner in the Atlas UI during the
Key Identifier rotation process. Do **not**
delete or disable the original Key Identifier until your
changes have deployed.

If the cluster uses [Cloud Backup](backup-cloud-provider), do **not** delete
or disable the original Key Identifier until you
validate that no snapshots used that key for encryption.

sections.heading: Related Topics
sections.body:
- To enable Encryption at Rest using your Key Management when deploying
  an Atlas cluster, see [Manage Your Own Encryption Keys](create-cluster-enable-encryption).

- To enable Encryption at Rest using your Key Management for an
  existing Atlas cluster, see
  [Encryption at Rest](scale-cluster-enable-encryption).

- To learn more about Encryption at Rest using your Key Management in
  Atlas, see [Encryption at Rest using Customer Key Management](security-kms-encryption).

- To learn more about MongoDB Encryption at Rest, see
  [Encryption at Rest](manual/core/security-encryption-at-rest) in
  the MongoDB server documentation.

- To learn more about Encryption at Rest with Cloud Backups, see [Storage Engine and Cloud Backup Encryption](encrypted-cloud-provider-snapshot).

- To enable customer key Management for Search Nodes, see
  [Enable Customer Key Management for Search Nodes](enable-search-node-encryption).
